meta.title: Creatinine Clearance Calculator — Cockcroft-Gault Formula | Health Calculators
meta.description: Calculate creatinine clearance with the Cockcroft-Gault formula. Age, weight, and serum creatinine in mg/dL or µmol/L. Kidney function classification. Free, instant, anonymous.

creatinineClearance.title: Creatinine Clearance Calculator
creatinineClearance.description: Estimate your creatinine clearance with the Cockcroft-Gault formula. Used to assess kidney function and adjust medication dosing.
creatinineClearance.incompleteHint: Enter age, weight, and serum creatinine to calculate your creatinine clearance.

hints.normal: Clearance ≥ 90 mL/min: kidney function is in the normal range. Stay well hydrated, avoid nephrotoxic drugs, and keep blood pressure and blood sugar under regular control.
hints.mild: Clearance 60–89 mL/min: mildly decreased. With risk factors (diabetes, hypertension), an annual check is reasonable. Ask your doctor whether any medication doses need adjustment.
hints.moderate: Clearance 30–59 mL/min: moderately decreased (CKD stage G3). Co-management with a nephrologist is helpful; medication doses should be reviewed and complications monitored.
hints.severe: Clearance 15–29 mL/min: severely decreased (CKD stage G4). Specialist nephrology care is needed. Discuss preparation for possible kidney replacement therapy.
hints.kidneyFailure: Clearance < 15 mL/min: kidney failure (CKD stage G5). Immediate nephrology referral; dialysis or transplantation usually become necessary.

creatinineClearance.howItWorksText: Cockcroft-Gault formula (Cockcroft & Gault, Nephron 1976): CrCl = ((140 − age) × weight in kg) / (72 × serum creatinine in mg/dL), multiplied by 0.85 for women. SI units are converted automatically (88.4 µmol/L = 1 mg/dL). The formula remains widely used for medication dose adjustment — modern estimates also use CKD-EPI 2021 (eGFR).
creatinineClearance.disclaimer: This calculation is an estimate, not a diagnosis. Cockcroft-Gault overestimates clearance in obesity and may be inaccurate at body composition extremes. Therapy and dosing decisions belong to your doctor or pharmacist.

faq.a: Creatinine clearance is a measure of how much blood the kidneys filter free of creatinine each minute. It serves as a surrogate marker for the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and reflects kidney function.

faq.q: How does the Cockcroft-Gault formula work?
faq.a: ((140 − age) × weight in kg) / (72 × serum creatinine in mg/dL). For women, multiply the result by 0.85. Output is in mL/min. The formula has been in clinical use since 1976 and still drives many drug-dosing recommendations.

In healthy young adults, clearance is roughly 90–140 mL/min. It drops by about 1 mL/min per year with age. Sustained values below 60 mL/min for more than 3 months meet criteria for chronic kidney disease (CKD).

faq.q: Cockcroft-Gault or CKD-EPI — which should I use?
faq.a: CKD-EPI 2021 is the recommended estimate of eGFR (e.g. KDIGO). Cockcroft-Gault gives the creatinine clearance — still required for many drug labels and older studies. The two complement each other; many clinicians compute both.
Women on average have less muscle mass and therefore lower creatinine production. Without the correction, the formula would systematically overestimate clearance in women.

faq.q: What does a value below 60 mL/min mean?
faq.a: Sustained < 60 mL/min for more than 3 months meets CKD stage G3 or worse. It warrants nephrology evaluation — common drivers are diabetes, hypertension, certain medications, or primary kidney disease.

Obesity (overestimation), severe underweight, pregnancy, amputations, muscle wasting, vegan diets with low creatinine, and acute kidney injury can all skew the result. Confirm with 24-h urine collection or eGFR when in doubt.
